# Technology Lifecycle Standard

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every technology in the organization's portfolio SHOULD have an assigned
lifecycle stage, and transitions between stages SHOULD be deliberate,
documented decisions. The lifecycle stages mirror JOS's own stage labels:
assess, trial, adopt, hold, and retire.

- **Assess**: The technology is being evaluated. No production use. A
  time-boxed evaluation is underway with defined criteria for promotion
  or rejection.
- **Trial**: The technology is in limited production use. A specific team
  is using it in a bounded scope to validate fitness.
- **Adopt**: The technology is approved for general use. Teams may select
  it without additional justification.
- **Hold**: The technology is in use but no longer recommended for new
  work. Existing usage is maintained; new adoption requires exception
  approval.
- **Retire**: The technology is being actively removed. A sunset plan
  exists with timeline, migration path, and responsible owner.

## What Good Looks Like

- Every technology in use has an assigned lifecycle stage. No technology
  exists in an ambiguous "we just use it" state.
- Adoption decisions are recorded as architecture decision records
  (per JOS-S09). The rationale for moving a technology to adopt, hold,
  or retire is documented, not assumed.
- Sunset plans exist for every technology in the retire stage. The plan
  includes a timeline, a migration target, affected systems, and an
  owner accountable for completion.
- Regular horizon-scanning occurs — at minimum quarterly — to identify
  technologies that should enter the assess stage and technologies
  currently in adopt that should move to hold.
- The technology portfolio is visible to all teams. Anyone can see
  which technologies are in which stage and why.
- Stage transitions require explicit approval at a defined authority
  level. Technologies do not drift into adopt through accumulated usage.

## Gap Analysis Guide

- **No lifecycle tracking.** Technologies are adopted by individual
  choice and accumulate without governance. The portfolio is unknown.
- **Lifecycle stages exist but are not maintained.** A radar or list
  was created once and never updated. It reflects a past state, not
  the current reality.
- **No sunset plans.** Technologies are placed on hold but never
  actively retired. The portfolio grows monotonically. Maintenance
  burden compounds.
- **No horizon-scanning.** New technologies enter the portfolio only
  when a developer introduces them bottom-up. There is no deliberate
  evaluation process.
- **Adoption is implicit.** Technologies reach widespread use without
  a recorded decision. Nobody can explain why the organization uses
  technology X instead of technology Y.
- **No authority model.** Anyone can effectively adopt a technology
  by using it in production. There is no gate between trial and adopt.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 manufacturing organization managed a portfolio of materials,
processes, and equipment with lifecycle stages — evaluating new alloys,
standardizing on proven processes, retiring obsolete machinery. A 2070
organization managing AI model providers, compute substrates, and
protocol standards faces the same governance challenge at higher speed.
Deliberate technology lifecycle management is not a software-era
invention; it is an organizational discipline that applies wherever
technology choices compound over time.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain standard extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer standards.

- JOS-C27 — technology lifecycle concept underpinning this standard
- JOS-C21 — the concept from which this standard also derives

  should be recorded as ADRs
